nativeRange.habitat: Rivers and streams from the lower Oyapock River basin to the Essequibo River basin; lower tidal-influenced river sections, quiet-flowing water with muddy substrate, and swamps.
nativeRange.biotope: Riverine/demersal; bottom-associated in slow to moderate current over muddy or rocky substrate.

decorPreferences: hardy plants (Java fern, Anubias — avoid soft-leafed plants which may be consumed)

diet.notes: Primarily plant-based diet; has been documented eating algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach, peas), sinking pellets, and driftwood (for dietary fiber and rasping behavior). Occasional protein supplementation with frozen bloodworms or gel food is generally advised. Does not subsist on aquarium algae or fish waste alone. Feeds nocturnally, so food should be offered in the evening.

goodWith: medium to large peaceful community fish
avoidWith: small fish that fit in mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Ayesha46; 4096x2120. Sharp full-body side view in aquarium over sand, showing the classic dark reticulated/vermiculated pattern, tall sail-like dorsal fin, and suckermouth — textbook Hypostomus plecostomus.
Andrey Butko; 4928x3264. In-tank underwater view of a live pleco showing the spotted body, erect dorsal fin and suckermouth; other fish in background but pleco is the clear subject.
Motu purkait; 3200x1440. Sharp full-body specimen with the tall sailfin dorsal fully erect and reticulated head/spotted flank clearly visible (photographed out of water).

summary: The Common Pleco is one of the most frequently abandoned fish in the aquarium hobby — juveniles are cheap and small, but adults grow to 40–50 cm and need a tank of at least 500 litres. Smaller pleco species are recommended for most hobbyists.
careNotes:
IMPORTANT: The Common Pleco is widely considered one of the most frequently abandoned and dumped aquarium fish in the hobby. Juveniles are mass-produced in Southeast Asia and sold at very low cost, which leads to widespread over-purchase by hobbyists who are not prepared for the adult size of 40–50 cm. Adults generally require a minimum of 500 litres and ideally 1,000 litres or more for a full-sized specimen.

For most hobbyists, smaller pleco alternatives are strongly recommended. The Bristlenose Pleco (Ancistrus spp.) reaches only around 15 cm and is manageable in a 100–120 litre tank. The Clown Pleco (Panaqolus maccus) stays under 10 cm and suits a 75–80 litre setup. The Rubber Lip Pleco (Chaetostoma spp.) reaches around 18 cm and needs roughly 150 litres. The Otocinclus (Otocinclus spp.) is a miniature algae grazer at about 5 cm, comfortable in 40 litres or more.

The Common Pleco is an established invasive species in Florida, Texas, and Hawaii, as well as other warm-water regions globally, as a result of aquarium dumping. It has been documented displacing native species, eroding river banks through burrowing, and altering habitat. Releasing this fish into open water is illegal in many jurisdictions and ecologically harmful.

The aquarium trade commonly sells numerous Hypostomus species — and related genera such as Pterygoplichthys — under the 'Common Pleco' label, most of which reach a similar adult size of 40–50 cm. Care guidance below applies to the hobby-trade animal across these species.

The tank requires robust filtration, as this species produces significant waste. Driftwood should be provided as a dietary supplement and to support the natural rasping behavior. Soft-leafed plants will generally be consumed; hardy species such as Java fern and Anubias are better choices. The Common Pleco is nocturnal by nature and is most active after lights out.

Copper-based medications are generally considered harmful to this species, as they are to most members of the family Loricariidae (the armored catfishes), despite their bony armor plates. Copper sensitivity should be treated as equivalent to that of a scaleless fish.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria has been reported but is considered difficult, primarily because of the scale of tank required. In the wild, the Common Pleco spawns in burrows dug into river banks. Cave-breeding behavior has been documented in captivity; the male guards the eggs through incubation. A reduction in water temperature of a few degrees Celsius is sometimes used to trigger spawning. Given the tank sizes involved, successful breeding is not a realistic goal for most home aquarium keepers.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source for the fish-freshwater slice. Covers Common Pleco within a broader pleco care guide. Tank size guidance: start in 75-gal (284L), ultimately needs 180–500+ gal (681–1893L). Temperature 74–80°F (23–27°C), pH 6.5–7.8. Emphasizes invasive status and recommends smaller alternatives.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Secondary source. Reports adult size 20–24 inches (~51–61 cm) in aquaria; lifespan 10–15 years; temperature 72–86°F (22–30°C); pH 6.5–8.0; hardness 20–35 dGH. Minimum tank 30 gal (114L) — likely understatement for retailers. gH/kH values were softer than controller key data; hardness range averaged with key data to produce final ranges.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic cross-reference. Confirms Hypostomus plecostomus (Linnaeus, 1758), Family Loricariidae, Order Siluriformes. Lists max size 25 cm SL / 28 cm TL for the wild Guianas population — significantly smaller than the hobby 'common pleco' which includes multiple Hypostomus spp. Temperature 20–28°C, pH 6.2–8.2, dH up to 28. Native range: Oyapock to Essequibo river basins. SeriouslyFish was inaccessible (HTTP 520); Practical Fishkeeping returned 403. FishBase used as third cross-check source.

fish.breedingNotes: The Common Pleco is a cave-breeding species in which the male guards the eggs. Successful spawning requires a large tank with suitable burrow or cave structure and is generally triggered by a moderate drop in water temperature. Breeding is rarely accomplished in home aquaria given the tank sizes this species demands.
